hook.text: If the strong nuclear force were 2% weaker, no atoms beyond hydrogen could exist. No stars, no life.

buildup.text: The universe's fundamental constants seem precisely tuned for complexity. Change any slightly and everything falls apart.

discovery.text: The cosmological constant is fine-tuned to 120 decimal places. A tiny change would make the universe collapse or fly apart.

twist.text: Some physicists invoke the multiverse—infinite universes with random constants. We exist in one that works.

climax.text: Others see fine-tuning as evidence of deeper physics we haven't discovered yet. The debate rages on.

punchline.text: Constants perfect for life. Coincidence, multiverse, or deeper law? Nobody knows.

quiz.question: What is the fine-tuning problem in physics?
options: The fundamental constants of nature appear precisely set for complex structures and life
The universe is expanding at exactly the speed of light
Gravity and electromagnetism have the same strength
